---
title: "The 'misfit' rater is a coin flip"
---

Chapter 3 is one of the more technically ambitious parts of the study: it builds
inaccuracy directly into the data-generating model rather than gesturing at it. This
chapter concerns a single modeling choice inside that design that, once adjusted,
strengthens the journal version. The relevant pages are 55–58.

## What the dissertation does

To create a "generally inaccurate" or misfitting rater, the simulation adds a rater
slope (discrimination) parameter $\alpha$ to the data-generating MFRM (Equation 7,
introduced p. 55). Normal raters get $\alpha = 1$ — the standard Rasch assumption.
Inaccurate raters take a decisive step: "we manipulated the slope parameter by **setting
its mean to 0**, whereas the slope parameter for the normal raters was fixed at 1"
(p. 57). The rationale is that "when raters have lower slopes, they exhibit less
discrimination between examinees" (p. 57), and this third condition is labeled "general
inaccuracy (i.e., misfit or haphazard ratings)" (p. 57). On the HRM side, "rater misfit
can be broadly interpreted as rater variability and is reflected in the psi parameter"
(p. 58).

The intuition is sound — a rater who cannot tell high-ability from low-ability examinees
is, in a real sense, inaccurate. The question is how far the design takes that intuition:
all the way to $\alpha = 0$.

## Why this threatens the conclusion

Consider what a slope of exactly zero does, worked out from Equation 7. In the
adjacent-category model, the log-odds of category $k$ over category $k-1$ is
$\alpha$ times the usual Rasch term. Set $\alpha = 0$ and **every** one of those
log-odds collapses to zero at once — which forces all five categories to probability
exactly $0.20$. The distribution is perfectly uniform, independent of the examinee's
ability, the item, and even the rater's own severity $\lambda$ (because $\lambda$ is
multiplied by $\alpha$, and anything times zero is zero). The rater is, quite literally,
rolling a five-sided die.

One point is worth stating plainly, because the design is a little more careful
than *every* misfit rater being a die. The slope *mean* is set to 0 — the misfit slopes
are drawn from a distribution centered at zero (almost certainly $\alpha \sim N(0, 0.05)$,
mirroring the normal raters' $N(1, 0.05)$), not pinned there. So $\alpha = 0$ is the
**expected**, typical misfit rater — the case the algebra above describes — and individual
misfit raters scatter around it: at the study's sample sizes their realized score-to-ability
correlations spread by roughly $\pm 0.4$ (largely finite-sample noise plus the small slope
spread), with a mean near zero. That refinement does not soften the point — it sharpens
it. Because the *mean* slope is 0, the condition is centered on the no-signal case: on
average these raters carry no ability signal, so the inflated fit is still the expected,
mechanical consequence — a positive control, not evidence of sensitivity to realistic
inaccuracy. The accurate claim is that the *typical* misfit rater is a coin flip, not that
every one is exactly uniform.

Equation 7 was re-implemented from scratch in R — not a re-run of TAM, but a
ground-up reconstruction — and confirmed. @fig-misfit-noise makes it concrete. Panel
A puts the two raters side by side: the accurate rater ($\alpha = 1$) produces a peaked,
ability-tracking distribution with a score-to-ability correlation of about **0.88**; the
"inaccurate" rater ($\alpha = 0$) produces five equal bars at $0.20$, with a correlation
of about **0.00**. These are not two points on a quality continuum — they are
qualitatively different things. Panel B sweeps the slope from 1 down to 0 and shows the
MFRM infit/outfit rising smoothly from $\approx 1.0$ to $\approx 3.5$–$4.3$, passing
straight through the dissertation's reported misfit band ($\approx 2.0$–$3.2$). The
inflated fit is a *mechanical consequence of the slope knob*, not a discovery.

```{r}
#| label: fig-misfit-noise
#| fig-cap: "A 'misfit' rater is a coin flip. **(A)** With the slope set to 0, the rater's five categories are essentially equally likely (≈ 0.20 each) and the score-to-ability correlation is ≈ 0 — versus a peaked, ability-tracking profile (r ≈ 0.88) for an accurate rater. **(B)** Turning the data-generating slope α from 1 (accurate) down to 0 (misfit) makes the MFRM fit mean-square rise smoothly from ≈ 1.0 through the dissertation's reported misfit band (≈ 2.0–3.2). The inflated fit is a deterministic function of the slope, not a finding."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4
#| code-summary: "Show the R code (implements the dissertation's Eq. 7 from scratch)"
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork) })
source("R/common.R")

set.seed(20260609)
N_EX <- 500L; I <- 5L; delta <- rnorm(I, 0, 0.5)

one_rater_observed <- function(theta, alpha, lam = 0) {
  obs <- matrix(0L, length(theta), I)
  for (i in seq_len(I)) obs[, i] <- simulate_rating(pcmfrm_probs(theta, lam, delta[i], alpha = alpha))
  obs
}
oracle_fit <- function(theta, observed) {              # scored vs the slope-1 analysis model
  z2 <- var <- numeric(0)
  for (i in seq_len(I)) {
    p <- pcmfrm_probs(theta, 0, delta[i], alpha = 1); ex <- expected_score(p); v <- pmax(score_variance(p), 1e-9)
    z2 <- c(z2, (observed[, i] - ex)^2 / v); var <- c(var, v)
  }
  list(infit = sum(z2 * var) / sum(var), outfit = mean(z2))
}

# ---- Panel A: accurate vs misfit category frequencies -----------------------
theta_A <- rnorm(N_EX)
obs_norm <- one_rater_observed(theta_A, 1); obs_mis <- one_rater_observed(theta_A, 0)
freq_norm <- tabulate(as.integer(obs_norm) + 1L, K) / length(obs_norm)
freq_mis  <- tabulate(as.integer(obs_mis)  + 1L, K) / length(obs_mis)
r_norm <- cor(rowSums(obs_norm), theta_A); r_mis <- cor(rowSums(obs_mis), theta_A)

dfA <- rbind(data.frame(rater = "Accurate rater (slope = 1)", k = factor(CATS), freq = freq_norm),
             data.frame(rater = "Misfit rater (slope = 0)",  k = factor(CATS), freq = freq_mis))
dfA$rater <- factor(dfA$rater, levels = c("Accurate rater (slope = 1)", "Misfit rater (slope = 0)"))
annA <- data.frame(rater = factor(levels(dfA$rater), levels = levels(dfA$rater)),
                   label = c(sprintf("r = %.2f", r_norm), sprintf("r = %.2f", r_mis)))
pA <- ggplot(dfA, aes(k, freq, fill = rater)) +
  geom_col(width = .7, colour = "white") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = .2, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40", linewidth = .7) +
  annotate("text", x = .65, y = .215, label = "Uniform 1/5", hjust = 0, size = 2.9,
           colour = "grey35", fontface = "italic") +
  geom_text(data = annA, aes(x = 4.45, y = .37, label = label), inherit.aes = FALSE,
            hjust = 1, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold",
            colour = c(unname(pal_companion["accent"]), unname(pal_companion["misfit"]))) +
  facet_wrap(~ rater) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Accurate rater (slope = 1)" = unname(pal_companion["accent"]),
                               "Misfit rater (slope = 0)"  = unname(pal_companion["misfit"])), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, .45), breaks = seq(0, .4, .1),
                     labels = c("0", ".10", ".20", ".30", ".40"), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "(A)  The 'misfit' rater is a coin-flip",
       subtitle = "slope = 0  →  every category equally likely; no signal about ability",
       x = "Rating category (0–4)", y = "Relative frequency") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9.5),
                              strip.background = element_blank(), panel.spacing = unit(1.2, "lines"),
                              plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

# ---- Panel B: fit MSE vs slope ----------------------------------------------
set.seed(20260610)
alphas <- seq(0, 1, .1); n_rep <- 60L
sweep <- do.call(rbind, lapply(alphas, function(a) {
  fr <- replicate(n_rep, { th <- rnorm(N_EX); ob <- one_rater_observed(th, a); f <- oracle_fit(th, ob)
                           c(f$infit, f$outfit, cor(rowSums(ob), th)) })
  data.frame(alpha = a, infit = mean(fr[1, ]), outfit = mean(fr[2, ]), r = mean(fr[3, ]))
}))
dfB <- rbind(data.frame(alpha = sweep$alpha, value = sweep$infit, stat = "Infit MSE"),
             data.frame(alpha = sweep$alpha, value = sweep$outfit, stat = "Outfit MSE"))
pB <- ggplot(dfB, aes(alpha, value, colour = stat)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = 2.0, ymax = 3.2, fill = "#C0392B", alpha = .08) +
  annotate("text", x = .82, y = 2.6, label = "dissertation's\nmisfit band\n(2.0–3.2)", hjust = 1,
           size = 2.8, colour = "#C0392B", lineheight = .95, fontface = "italic") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", linewidth = .6) +
  annotate("text", x = .9, y = 1.28, label = "expected fit = 1.0", hjust = 1, size = 2.9,
           colour = "grey45", fontface = "italic") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  annotate("text", x = .97, y = .55, label = sprintf("r = %.2f", sweep$r[sweep$alpha == 1]),
           hjust = 1, size = 3, colour = "grey30", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = .03, y = .55, label = sprintf("r = %.2f", sweep$r[sweep$alpha == 0]),
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "grey30", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Infit MSE" = unname(pal_companion["misfit"]),
                                              "Outfit MSE" = unname(pal_companion["severe"]))) +
  scale_x_reverse(breaks = seq(0, 1, .2), labels = c("0\n(misfit)", ".2", ".4", ".6", ".8", "1\n(accurate)")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 6), breaks = 0:6, expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "(B)  'Inaccuracy' is just a signal-to-noise knob",
       subtitle = "MFRM fit rises deterministically as the slope drops — a positive control, not evidence of sensitivity",
       x = "Data-generating slope α (rater discrimination)", y = "Fit MSE (infit / outfit)") +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

pA | pB
```

Three threats follow, and they compound.

**Detection is trivialized.** A pure-noise rater whose scores carry *zero* information is
not a realistic model of human inaccuracy. Real raters who are inconsistent, fatigued, or
idiosyncratic still have *some* slope — they discriminate imperfectly, not randomly. So
the reported detection rates are for an extreme case any reasonable index can flag, not
for the realistic inaccuracy a reviewer cares about.

**Severity becomes unidentifiable.** Because $\lambda$ is multiplied by zero, the severity
of an $\alpha = 0$ rater has no influence on the data and cannot be estimated. This is the
*same* manipulation that, in Chapter 1, leaves the HRM's $\psi$ with no finite value — so
Chapters 1 and 3 are two faces of one design choice. Naming that link explicitly
strengthens both.

**The MFRM has a home-field advantage.** Infit and outfit measure deviation from the Rasch
model — and the misfit data were made by taking a Rasch/MFRM structure and *erasing* it
($\alpha = 0$). Asking the MFRM to notice that its own structure has been removed is not a
neutral test of detection power; it is a model recognizing its own data. A reviewer will
see this at once.

And the advantage is not confined to these misfit cells — it runs through all of Study 2.
Every condition, None and Severity no less than Misfit, is generated from the same adapted
MFRM of Equation 7; the fitted MFRM is that very model with the slope switched off, while
the HRM, whose first stage is a GPCM, must recover from a rival model's data. So the MFRM is
always playing at home. The dissertation anticipated exactly this: its Purpose
states that "*rather than comparing the two models directly*, we aim to examine the
different patterns each model exhibits under various conditions" (p. 48), and that framing
genuinely defuses the concern — a study of how *each* model behaves on its own terms is not
a rigged head-to-head. The catch is that the conclusions
still read comparatively in places — which model is *more* sensitive, and where — and the
instant a comparison is drawn, the home-field tilt is back. The clean way to keep the
comparative payoff without the tilt is the cross-generative design already in the Fix-it:
generate from each model and score with both, so any remaining comparison is symmetric and
neither model is graded on its home turf.

There is also an internal tension. The dissertation's review chapter stresses that
fit statistics are **non-specific** — a high infit/outfit can come from inconsistency,
halo, central tendency, range restriction, criterion bias, or model misspecification, not
only from haphazard noise (the dissertation says so, p. 58). Yet Chapters 3–4 read a
*rise in fit* directly as "inaccuracy detection." Under known simulation truth that is
fine; but it means the detection claim is conditional on the truth being pure-noise —
exactly the specificity the review warns cannot be assumed in practice.

::: {.callout-note}
## The strongest counterargument

Setting the misfit slope mean to 0 was not an oversight — it was a
*deliberate, clean stress test*. To verify that the fit indices can detect inaccuracy at
all, the disciplined first move is to hand them the most extreme, least ambiguous case and
confirm they fire; if an index failed even *here*, it would fail everywhere. Choosing the
limiting case to establish that floor is sound design in the tradition of a positive
control, not a belief that real raters roll dice.

And yet — $\alpha = 0$ is a legitimate
*positive control*, and Panel B's smooth slope-to-fit curve is genuinely reassuring. What a
positive control establishes, though, is only that the index fires in the limit; it cannot,
by itself, license the broader claim that the indices are sensitive to the inaccuracy raters
show *in practice* — halo, central tendency, partial mis-discrimination ($\alpha = 0.2, 0.5,
0.8$), criterion bias. $\alpha = 0$ stays as the anchor; the revision adds those
realistic mechanisms alongside it and scopes the conclusion to match — "the indices flag a
pure-noise rater" is proven now, "the indices detect realistic inaccuracy" needs the
realistic cases to earn it.
:::

## What would fix it

::: {.callout-tip}
## Fix-it
- **Widen the inaccuracy operationalization.** Replace the single $\alpha = 0$ point with
  partial reductions ($\alpha = 0.2, 0.5, 0.8$) *and* mechanisms drawn from the
  literature the review synthesizes: threshold/category-usage distortion, central
  tendency, extremity, criterion- or subgroup-specific bias, and mixture (occasional)
  noise. These give *detection curves* over realistic raters, not one extreme point.
- **Use a neutral, cross-generative design.** Generate from a third model that is neither
  the MFRM nor the HRM (or cross the two symmetrically — generate from each, score with
  both), so neither model is graded on its home turf and a genuine comparison is possible.
- **Reconcile with the non-specificity point.** Add a Discussion sentence acknowledging
  that elevated fit is *consistent with* — not *diagnostic of* — inaccuracy versus other
  effects, and frame the rates as "sensitivity to a pure-noise pattern."
- **Scope the current result honestly.** One sentence: the present finding shows the
  indices can flag a rater whose scores are informationally empty. That is real and
  useful — it just needs the right frame.
:::

## What a reviewer will ask

::: {.callout-warning}
## What a reviewer will ask
"Is a slope of zero — pure uniform noise — a valid operationalization of 'general
inaccuracy'? Human raters who are inattentive or inconsistent still carry *some*
information about examinees. And since infit/outfit measure deviation from the very Rasch
model that generated the data, how do you separate the MFRM's structural advantage from a
genuine finding — would the same rates hold under a model-neutral generating process?"
:::

A careful reviewer will not be hostile here — they will likely admire the design and want
its bounding conditions. The strongest move is to preempt them: state that $\alpha = 0$ is
an extreme case, show (Panel B) that the slope-to-fit relationship is smooth and
continuous, and offer the cross-generative extension. Reviewers reward authors who have
already thought the critique through — and the revisions above do exactly that.
